# Command Cheat Sheet {.unnumbered}

A one-page reference for the commands taught in this book. Nothing here
is new: each command is explained in the chapter noted beside it. Modern
Git throughout (`git switch` / `git restore`, not `git checkout`).

## One-time setup {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 4](chapters/04-install-setup.qmd).

```bash
# identity (used in every commit)
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.edu"

# make new repos start on main
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# inspect your config
git config --global --list
```

## The daily loop {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 5](chapters/05-git-beginners.qmd).

```bash
git status -s            # what changed (short form)
git diff                 # unstaged changes
git add <file>           # stage a file
git add -p               # stage selected hunks
git diff --staged        # what you are about to commit
git commit -m "message"  # commit the staged snapshot
git log --oneline        # compact history
```

## Undo, safely {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 5](chapters/05-git-beginners.qmd) and
[Chapter 17](chapters/17-disasters-recovery.qmd).

```bash
git restore <file>          # discard unstaged edits
git restore --staged <file> # unstage, keep edits
git commit --amend          # fix the last commit
git revert <commit>         # undo a commit with a new commit
git reset --soft HEAD~1     # uncommit, keep changes staged
git reflog                  # find "lost" commits (90 days)
```

## Branching and history {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 7](chapters/07-git-advanced.qmd).

```bash
git switch -c <branch>   # create and switch to a branch
git switch <branch>      # switch to an existing branch
git merge <branch>       # merge a branch into the current one
git merge --abort        # back out of a conflicted merge
git stash                # shelve work in progress
git rebase -i <base>     # tidy local commits before sharing
git bisect start         # binary-search for a breaking commit
git cherry-pick <commit> # apply one commit here
git log --oneline --graph
```

## Tags and releases {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 7](chapters/07-git-advanced.qmd) and
[Chapter 15](chapters/15-replication-package.qmd).

```bash
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "..."  # annotated, named version
git tag -n                  # list tags with messages
git show v1.0.0 --stat      # what a tag points at
git tag -d v1.0.0           # delete a local tag (pre-push)
```

## What must never be committed {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 6](chapters/06-never-commit.qmd).

```bash
# keep data, secrets, and noise out via .gitignore
git status --ignored -s     # confirm they are ignored
git rm --cached <file>      # stop tracking an added file

# last resort: purge a secret from ALL history
git filter-repo --invert-paths --path <leaked-file>
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`git filter-repo` rewrites history and changes commit hashes for
everyone. It is a recovery tool, not a daily one. See
[Chapter 6](chapters/06-never-commit.qmd) before using it, and rotate any
exposed secret regardless.
:::

## Remotes: Git to GitHub {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 9](chapters/09-git-to-github.qmd).

```bash
git remote add origin <url> # link a remote
git remote -v               # list remotes
git push -u origin main     # first push (sets upstream)
git push                    # send later commits
git fetch                   # download, do not merge
git pull                    # fetch + merge
git clone <url>             # copy a repo locally
```

## Inspecting without changing {.unnumbered}

Read-only commands are always safe to run while you orient yourself.

```bash
git status         # working-tree state
git log            # commit history
git diff           # unstaged changes
git show           # one commit or tag in detail
git remote -v      # configured remotes
git push --dry-run # what a push would send
```

See the [reproducibility checklist](checklist.qmd) for the pre-submission
pass that ties these together.
